title: Alfred Stevens (painter)

infobox.value: Alfred Émile Léopold Stevens  (1823-05-11)11 May 1823  [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), [United Kingdom of the Netherlands](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "United Kingdom of the Netherlands")

*[What is Called Vagrancy](/wiki/What_is_Called_Vagrancy "What is Called Vagrancy")*, *[The Psyché](/wiki/The_Psych%C3%A9_(My_Studio) "The Psyché (My Studio)")*, *[The Parisian Sphinx](/wiki/The_Parisian_Sphinx "The Parisian Sphinx")*

[Social realism](/wiki/Social_realism "Social realism"), [Realism](/wiki/Realism_(arts) "Realism (arts)"), [Orientalism](/wiki/Orientalism "Orientalism"), [Dutch realism style](/wiki/Dutch_Golden_Age_painting "Dutch Golden Age painting")

(11 May 1823 – 24 August 1906) was a [Belgian](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") painter, known for his paintings of elegant modern women. In their realistic style and careful finish, his works reveal the influence of 17th-century Dutch [genre](/wiki/Genre_works "Genre works") painting. After gaining attention early in his career with a [social realist](/wiki/Social_realist "Social realist") painting depicting the plight of poor vagrants, he achieved great critical and popular success with his scenes of upper-middle class Parisian life.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

19th-century Belgian painter

| Alfred Stevens | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Born | Alfred Émile Léopold Stevens  (1823-05-11)11 May 1823  [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), [United Kingdom of the Netherlands](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "United Kingdom of the Netherlands") |
| Died | 24 August 1906(1906-08-24) (aged 83)  [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), [France](/wiki/France "France") |
| Known for | [Painting](/wiki/Painting "Painting") |
| Notable work | *[What is Called Vagrancy](/wiki/What_is_Called_Vagrancy "What is Called Vagrancy")*, *[The Psyché](/wiki/The_Psych%C3%A9_(My_Studio) "The Psyché (My Studio)")*, *[The Parisian Sphinx](/wiki/The_Parisian_Sphinx "The Parisian Sphinx")* |
| Movement | [Social realism](/wiki/Social_realism "Social realism"), [Realism](/wiki/Realism_(arts) "Realism (arts)"), [Orientalism](/wiki/Orientalism "Orientalism"), [Dutch realism style](/wiki/Dutch_Golden_Age_painting "Dutch Golden Age painting") |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Alfred_Stevens_Le_Bain.jpg/250px-Alfred_Stevens_Le_Bain.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alfred_Stevens_Le_Bain.jpg)

*[Le Bain](/wiki/The_Bath_(Stevens) "The Bath (Stevens)")*, 1873–74

**Alfred Émile Léopold Stevens** (11 May 1823 – 24 August 1906) was a [Belgian](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") painter, known for his paintings of elegant modern women. In their realistic style and careful finish, his works reveal the influence of 17th-century Dutch [genre](/wiki/Genre_works "Genre works") painting. After gaining attention early in his career with a [social realist](/wiki/Social_realist "Social realist") painting depicting the plight of poor vagrants, he achieved great critical and popular success with his scenes of upper-middle class Parisian life.

## Family background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Stevens_(painter)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Family background")]

Stevens was born in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"). He came from a family involved with the visual arts: his older brother [Joseph](/wiki/Joseph_Stevens_(painter) "Joseph Stevens (painter)") (1816–1892) and his son Léopold (1866–1935) were painters, while another brother Arthur (1825–1899) was an art dealer and critic. His father, who had fought in the Napoleonic wars in the army of [William I of the Netherlands](/wiki/William_I_of_the_Netherlands "William I of the Netherlands"), was an art collector who owned several watercolors by [Eugène Delacroix](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_Delacroix "Eugène Delacroix"), among other artists. His mother's parents ran Café de l'Amitié in Brussels, a meeting place for politicians, writers, and artists. All the Stevens children benefited from the people they met there, and the social skills they acquired in growing up around important people.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Stevens_(painter)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Education")]

After the death of his father in 1837, Stevens left middle school to begin study at the [Académie Royale des Beaux-Arts](/wiki/Acad%C3%A9mie_Royale_des_Beaux-Arts "Académie Royale des Beaux-Arts") in Brussels, where he knew [François Navez](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Navez "François Navez"), the Neo-Classical painter and former student of [Jacques-Louis David](/wiki/Jacques-Louis_David "Jacques-Louis David") who was its director and an old friend of Stevens's grandfather. Following a traditional curriculum, he drew from casts of classical sculpture for the first two years, and then drew from live models.[[2]](#cite_note-Stevens2009_p12-2) In 1843, Stevens went to [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), joining his brother Joseph who already was there. He was admitted to the [École des Beaux-Arts](/wiki/%C3%89cole_des_Beaux-Arts "École des Beaux-Arts"), the most important art school in Paris. Although it is said that he became a student of its director [Jean Auguste Dominique Ingres](/wiki/Jean_Auguste_Dominique_Ingres "Jean Auguste Dominique Ingres"), this is likely not true.[[2]](#cite_note-Stevens2009_p12-2) An early picture by Stevens, *The Pardon* or *Absolution* (Hermitage, St. Petersburg), signed and dated 1849, shows his mastery of a conventional naturalistic style which owes much to 17th-century Dutch [genre](/wiki/Genre_works "Genre works") painting. Like the Belgian painter and friend with whom he stayed in Paris, [Florent Willems](/wiki/Florent_Willems "Florent Willems") (1823–1905), Stevens carefully studied works by painters such as [Gerard ter Borch](/wiki/Gerard_ter_Borch "Gerard ter Borch") and [Gabriel Metsu](/wiki/Gabriel_Metsu "Gabriel Metsu").[[3]](#cite_note-Thomas-3)

## Career beginnings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Stevens_(painter)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Career beginnings")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Alfred_stevens%2C_quello_che_si_dice_vagabondaggio_%28i_cacciatori_di_vincennes%29%2C_1854.JPG/250px-Alfred_stevens%2C_quello_che_si_dice_vagabondaggio_%28i_cacciatori_di_vincennes%29%2C_1854.JPG)](/wiki/File:Alfred_stevens,_quello_che_si_dice_vagabondaggio_(i_cacciatori_di_vincennes),_1854.JPG)

*[What is Called Vagrancy](/wiki/What_is_Called_Vagrancy "What is Called Vagrancy")*, 1854

Stevens's work was shown publicly for the first time in 1851, when three of his paintings were admitted to the [Brussels Salon](/wiki/Brussels_Salon "Brussels Salon"). He was awarded a third-class medal at the [Paris Salon of 1853](/wiki/Salon_of_1853 "Salon of 1853"), and a second-class medal at the [Salon of 1855](/wiki/Salon_of_1855 "Salon of 1855") during the [Universal Exposition](/wiki/Exposition_Universelle_(1855) "Exposition Universelle (1855)") in Paris in 1855.[[3]](#cite_note-Thomas-3) His *[Ce qu'on appelle le vagabondage [What is called vagrancy]](/wiki/What_is_Called_Vagrancy "What is Called Vagrancy")* ([Musée d'Orsay](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_d%27Orsay "Musée d'Orsay"), Paris) attracted the attention of [Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III") who, as a result of the scene in the picture, ordered that soldiers no longer be used to pick up the poor from the streets.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Two other paintings he exhibited at the [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") Salon that year, *Chez soi* or *At Home* (present location unknown) and *The Painter and his Model* (Walters Art Museum, Baltimore), introduced subjects from "la vie moderne" for which he became known: an elegant young woman in contemporary dress and the artist in his studio. In 1857, Stevens made his first important sale to a private collector, when *Consolation* was bought for a rumored 6,000 francs by the Berlin collector and dealer Ravéné. At the same time, he and his brother were becoming part of the art world of Paris, meeting people such as the [Goncourt](/wiki/Goncourt "Goncourt") brothers, [Théophile Gautier](/wiki/Th%C3%A9ophile_Gautier "Théophile Gautier"), and [Alexandre Dumas](/wiki/Alexandre_Dumas "Alexandre Dumas") at the salons of [Princess Mathilde](/wiki/Mathilde_Bonaparte "Mathilde Bonaparte") as well as popular cafés.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/StevensMemories.jpg/250px-StevensMemories.jpg)](/wiki/File:StevensMemories.jpg)

*Memories and Regrets*, ca. 1874, [Clark Art Institute](/wiki/Clark_Art_Institute "Clark Art Institute"), [Williamstown](/wiki/Williamstown,_Massachusetts "Williamstown, Massachusetts")

In 1858, Stevens married Fanny Juliette Albertine Marie Hortense Blanc (1836–1891), who came from a rich Belgian family. Eugène Delacroix was a witness at the ceremony.[[5]](#cite_note-5)
The couple would have four children together—Leopold, Jean, Catherine, and Pierre. Stevens painted his wife's portrait many times, including *Memories and Regrets* (ca. 1874).

## Mature career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Stevens_(painter)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Mature career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Alfred_Stevens_-_La_Parisienne_japonaise.JPG/250px-Alfred_Stevens_-_La_Parisienne_japonaise.JPG)](/wiki/File:Alfred_Stevens_-_La_Parisienne_japonaise.JPG)

*[La parisienne japonaise](/wiki/La_parisienne_japonaise "La parisienne japonaise")*, 1872, Musée d'art moderne et d'art contemporain, [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Stevens%2C_Alfred%2C_The_Psych%C3%A9_%28My_Studio%29%2C_ca._1871.jpg/250px-Stevens%2C_Alfred%2C_The_Psych%C3%A9_%28My_Studio%29%2C_ca._1871.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stevens,_Alfred,_The_Psych%C3%A9_(My_Studio),_ca._1871.jpg)

*[The Psyché (My Studio)](/wiki/The_Psych%C3%A9_(My_Studio) "The Psyché (My Studio)")*, ca. 1871, [Princeton University Art Museum](/wiki/Princeton_University_Art_Museum "Princeton University Art Museum")

During the 1860s, Stevens became an immensely successful painter, known for his paintings of elegant modern women. His exhibits at the Salons in Paris and Brussels attracted favorable critical attention and buyers. An excellent example of his work during this time is *La Dame en Rose* or *Woman in Pink* ([Royal Museums of Fine Arts of Belgium](/wiki/Royal_Museums_of_Fine_Arts_of_Belgium "Royal Museums of Fine Arts of Belgium"), Brussels), painted in 1866, which combines a view of a fashionably dressed woman in an interior with a detailed examination of Japanese objects, a fashionable taste called [Japonisme](/wiki/Japonisme "Japonisme") of which Stevens was an early enthusiast.[[3]](#cite_note-Thomas-3) He tended to use the same models over and over again, and not all of them were aristocratic. "At least three of his frequent models can be identified in the infamous Book of the Courtesans, a top secret leather bound book containing the surveillance files of the Paris vice squad", writes author Summer Brennan.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In 1863, he received the [Legion of Honor](/wiki/Legion_of_Honor "Legion of Honor") (Chevalier) from the French government. In 1867, he won a first-class medal at the [Universal Exposition](/wiki/Exposition_Universelle_(1867) "Exposition Universelle (1867)") in Paris, where he and [Jan August Hendrik Leys](/wiki/Jan_August_Hendrik_Leys "Jan August Hendrik Leys") were the stars of the Belgian section, and was promoted to [Officer of the Legion of Honor](/wiki/Officer_of_the_Legion_of_Honor "Officer of the Legion of Honor"). His friends included [Édouard Manet](/wiki/%C3%89douard_Manet "Édouard Manet"), [Edgar Degas](/wiki/Edgar_Degas "Edgar Degas"), [Charles Baudelaire](/wiki/Charles_Baudelaire "Charles Baudelaire"), [Berthe Morisot](/wiki/Berthe_Morisot "Berthe Morisot"), [James Abbott McNeill Whistler](/wiki/James_Abbott_McNeill_Whistler "James Abbott McNeill Whistler"), [Frédéric Bazille](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Bazille "Frédéric Bazille"), and [Puvis de Chavannes](/wiki/Puvis_de_Chavannes "Puvis de Chavannes"), and he was a regular in the group that gathered at the Café Guerbois in Paris.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Stevens fought for the French during the siege of Paris in the [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War"), but returned to Belgium with his wife and family before the [Paris Commune](/wiki/Paris_Commune "Paris Commune"). They returned after the war, and Stevens continued to achieve critical acclaim as well as great success with collectors. In 1875, he bought a grand house and garden in Paris on rue des Martyrs, which appeared in his paintings as well as those of other artists, including Édouard Manet's *The Croquet Party* (Städel Museum, Frankfurt am Main) from 1873.[[7]](#cite_note-7) (He had to leave the house in 1880, however, to make way for the construction of a new street, which was named after him.) In 1878, he was made a Commander of the Legion of Honor and received another first-class medal at the Salon.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Despite earning a considerable income through the sale of his paintings, Stevens found that a combination of bad investments and excessive spending caused him great financial difficulties during the 1880s. An additional expense came from summers by the sea, which a doctor told Stevens in 1880 were essential for his health. Thus the artist was glad to agree when the Paris dealer Georges Petit offered him 50,000 francs to finance his vacation in exchange for the paintings Stevens produced during that time.[[8]](#cite_note-8) This deal, which lasted for three years, resulted in the sea becoming an important subject for him, and over the rest of his career, he painted hundreds of views of popular resorts along the Normandy coast and the Midi in the south. Many of them are painted in a sketchy style that shows the influence of the Impressionists. Stevens also began to take private students, including [Sarah Bernhardt](/wiki/Sarah_Bernhardt "Sarah Bernhardt"),[[3]](#cite_note-Thomas-3) who became a close personal friend, and [William Merritt Chase](/wiki/William_Merritt_Chase "William Merritt Chase"). Other students were [Berthe Art](/wiki/Berthe_Art "Berthe Art"), [Charles Bell Birch](/wiki/Charles_Bell_Birch "Charles Bell Birch"), [Jules Cayron](/w/index.php?title=Jules_Cayron&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jules Cayron (page does not exist)"), [Marie Collart-Henrotin](/wiki/Marie_Collart-Henrotin "Marie Collart-Henrotin"), [Louise De Hem](/wiki/Louise_De_Hem "Louise De Hem"), [Harriet Campbell Foss](/wiki/Harriet_Campbell_Foss "Harriet Campbell Foss"),[[9]](#cite_note-Tufts(U.S.)1987-9) [Rosalie Lorraine Gill](/wiki/Rosalie_Lorraine_Gill "Rosalie Lorraine Gill"),[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Georgette Meunier](/wiki/Georgette_Meunier "Georgette Meunier"), [Lilla Cabot Perry](/wiki/Lilla_Cabot_Perry "Lilla Cabot Perry"), [Jean-Paul Sinibaldi](/wiki/Jean-Paul_Sinibaldi "Jean-Paul Sinibaldi"), and [Fernand Toussaint](/wiki/Fernand_Toussaint "Fernand Toussaint").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Late career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Stevens_(painter)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Late career")]

The single most important work from the second half of Stevens's career is the monumental *Panorama du Siècle, 1789–1889*, which he painted with [Henri Gervex](/wiki/Henri_Gervex "Henri Gervex"). Stevens painted the women and details and Gervex the men, with the help of fifteen assistants.[[12]](#cite_note-12) It was shown to great acclaim at the International Exhibition held in Paris in 1889. He also received several great professional tributes. In 1895, a large exhibition of his work was held in Brussels. In 1900, Stevens was honored by the Ecole des Beaux-Arts in Paris with the first retrospective exhibition ever given to a living artist. Supported by patrons led by the Comtesse de Greffulhe, it achieved social cachet as well as popular success. In 1905, he was the only living artist allowed to exhibit in a retrospective show of Belgian art in Brussels. Despite these exhibitions, he was not able to sell enough of his work to manage well financially. Having outlived his brothers and most of his friends, he died in Paris in 1906, living alone in modest rooms, whilst still remaining in touch with his four children.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Honours and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Stevens_(painter)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Honours and legacy")]

* **1881**: Grand Officer in the [Order of Leopold](/wiki/Order_of_Leopold_(Belgium) "Order of Leopold (Belgium)").[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* Rue Alfred Stevens, a street off [Rue des Martyrs](/wiki/Rue_des_Martyrs "Rue des Martyrs") in the [9th arrondissement of Paris](/wiki/9th_arrondissement_of_Paris "9th arrondissement of Paris"), is named after him.

## Selected works

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Stevens_(painter)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Selected works")]
